point_summary: Revelation 4:1's "come up hither" is read as a picture of the rapture—the moment believers are caught up to heaven before the things that shall be hereafter unfold.

context: I looked and behold, a door was opened in heaven. And the first voice which I heard was as it were of a trumpet talking with me, which said, come up hither
point_summary: Revelation 4:1's "come up hither" is read as a picture of the rapture, the moment believers are caught up to stand before the throne John immediately sees in heaven.

context: I was in the spirit and behold, a throne was set in heaven and one sat on the throne
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the four and 20 elders fall down before him
point_summary: Worship in heaven is depicted as a corporate, responsive act — the twenty-four elders fall down and cast their crowns before the throne in direct response to the four living creatures giving glory to God.

context: when those beasts give glory
point_summary: Revelation 4:9 functions as the trigger event: the living creatures' giving of glory, honor, and thanks to the eternal God on the throne is what prompts the twenty-four elders to fall down in worship.

context: thou art worthy, oh Lord, to receive glory and honor and power, for thou hast created all things and for thy pleasure they are and were created
point_summary: Revelation 4:11's declaration "Thou art worthy" is presented as a conclusion the believer must personally reach, grounded in God's role as Creator of all things for His own pleasure.

context: they sung a new song saying, thou art worthy to take the book and to open the seals thereof for thou was slain and has redeemed us to God by thy blood out of every kindred and tongue and people and nation and has made us unto our God kings and priests
point_summary: The phrase "thou art worthy" in Revelation 5:9 is cited alongside Revelation 4:11 to show that both the Father and the slain, redeeming Christ share the same ascription of worthiness in heaven's worship.

context: as we come to the text of scripture and finish this scene in Revelation chapter number four, that the Bible says thou art worthy, O Lord, and then it says this, to receive, to receive
point_summary: The repeated declaration "thou art worthy" in Revelation 4:11 and 5:9 frames worship as the acknowledgment that God and Christ deserve to receive everything, with the English word "worship" itself rooted in the concept of worthship.

context: at the end of verse number 10 as they cast their crowns before the throne
point_summary: Worship is rightly owed to God because He is worthy—a truth the elders demonstrate by casting their crowns before the throne, and one every believer must personally acknowledge in their own walk with God.

context: John is caught up, he hears that voice as a trumpet come up hither
point_summary: John's vision of the open door in heaven—"come up hither"—frames the entire throne-room scene of Revelation 4 as the vantage point from which worship and worthiness are witnessed.

context: Thou art worthy, O Lord
point_summary: Revelation 4:11's declaration that God is worthy "to receive" glory reframes the word *receive* away from human reception of salvation toward God himself as the recipient of worship owed to him.

context: we enter into Revelation four and five and what a scene it is
point_summary: In contrast to the believer's receiving of salvation, this passage redirects attention to God as the one receiving—specifically glory, honor, and power from those who worship Him.

context: thou art worthy O Lord to receive
point_summary: Revelation 4:11 reframes the usual direction of receiving: God Himself is the recipient, and the question pressed is what glory, honor, and power believers are actually giving Him.

context: Revelation 4 and verse number 11, and we see that the Bible says that he is worthy. That when these four beasts stand and they begin to proclaim and to worship, and when the four and twenty elders representing the redeemed, as we would fall down before him, and it's this proclamation, thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honor and power. For thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created.
point_summary: Worship is owed to God not on the basis of favorable circumstances but because He is inherently worthy as Creator and Sustainer, as the elders' proclamation in this verse declares.

context: He is Alpha and Omega
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: thou art worthy to receive
point_summary: The heavenly declaration that God is "worthy to receive" becomes a searching question about what praise and gratitude each believer is actually offering God in daily life.

context: he is the one that searches the hearts
point_summary: God alone searches and knows the human heart, a truth affirmed by Christ's own claim in Revelation 2:23 and echoed in Jeremiah 17:10.
